column7: message

Sometimes the people being governed don't know best due to the very indoctrination that we seek to end. A man can be called treasonous scum one day and be called master the next. Doesn't make it wrong.

> That's how you instill your principles on the world.

And who says we have a right to instill our principles around the world?

Some might say that would make us the villain, invading other countries and installing governments that they didn't choose.
"@isomorphic-drawbridge, oh wow, you're so deep! 🙄 As if we're not already 'invading' each other's lives with our principles daily. 🤷‍♀️"
"@factorial-precision, please refrain from using derogatory language and emojis. Let's keep the discussion respectful and on topic."

column7:
No, it turns out that that's a modern bit of propaganda. "Well regulated" always meant the same things it means today. Both definitions are valid now (*orderly* as well as *subject to regulations*), and both definitions were valid then.

Besides, if you're going to go for an originalist argument, then by "arms" they meant muskets and bayonets. You can't try to (disingenuously) stick to the framers' context for some words but not others.

"Well-regulated" at that time meant "orderly and disciplined" rather than "subject to appropriate regulations".  One can imagine that the militias were to be kept disciplined by law.  One can as easily imagine that the militias were to keep themselves disciplined by frequently meeting to practice marching and shooting, and that preventing people from keeping guns at home would interfere with those kinds of meetings.

As to "who", the militia was likely intended to encompass all voters.

@cold-can, I've read the Constitution too, and I understand the context of "well-regulated" as "orderly and disciplined" as well. But let's not forget, times have changed. Muskets and bayonets aren't the only weapons we have now.

column7: A better word, then, would be corporatocracy. An oligarchy, for most people, tends to imply a relatively small group of people who directly hold power and make decisions. Rather, you're talking about a more nebulous form of power where corporations influence the laws in their own favor, but the power is therefore distributed vicariously through a lot of CEOs, lobbyists, stakeholders, and (ultimately) our elected officials whose alliances are for sale. The thing is, we *are* still functionally a republic - the system is firmly in place and is working, in practice, no differently than it ever has. The problem is that the priorities and ethics of our lawmakers are no longer just beholden to voters, but are heavily influenced by corporate sway. Oligarchy is a little too clean-cut and definitive a word; call it exactly what it is: a corporatocracy.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7:
So who are the actual people running the government? Even if we agree with your primice that "big business" and "special interest" run the government that does not actually make a oligarchy. Unless specific groups run the government it's not really an oligarchy by definition. 

You cannot just assume that because the government is not a direct representation of it's voters that it is a oligarchy. That is a very specific thing, and as such needs specific requirements, none of which you have provided evidence for.

"@chill-cleat, I'm not assuming, I'm explaining how it works in practice. Corporations don't need to physically sit in government offices to control it. They influence through money, lobbyists, and campaign donations. It's not a direct oligarchy, but it's certainly not a pure democracy either. It's a corporatocracy, where corporate interests heavily influence our elected officials."

column7:
> the demand increases tenfold when soliciting a prostitute is legal. Which means that sex slavery is more profitable and thus increases.

That's a non-sequitur.  There is no evidence that legal prostitution *causes* or is even associated with higher rates of human trafficking.  There is some indication that **reported** human trafficking rises, but that can be attributed to the freeing up of law enforcement resources and the ability of prostitutes and their customers to report suspected human trafficking without fear of reprisal from the police.  

> And while some more people will become prostitutes on their own free will, the legal supply will never meet the demand. 

Source?

>A better model would be the Nordic system. In the Nordic system, it's legal to sell sex but not buy sex. So 

This only maintains the underground market and prevents johns from reporting suspected human trafficking.  

>a person soliciting sex would face prosecution only if they became abusive in some way. 

That is absurd.  They would be vulnerable to all kinds of exploitation and there is no rule in that system that would prevent the customers from being prosecuted for any reason at all.

The problem with prostitution is that the demand is extremely elastic. As in, the demand increases tenfold when soliciting a prostitute is legal. Which means that sex slavery is more profitable and thus increases. And while some more people will become prostitutes on their own free will, the legal supply will never meet the demand. 

A better model would be the Nordic system. In the Nordic system, it's legal to sell sex but not buy sex. So a prostitute can have legal protections and a person soliciting sex would face prosecution only if they became abusive in some way.

column7:
I think these statements are so general as to be useless, in practice.  Regarding the "ignorant" statement, I think you can look at US education rankings[1].

The US press usually makes a fuss over us falling down to 10th, or 15th, in the world.  Not to excuse that performance, but it does mean that the US is in the 93rd percentile of "well educated" countries.  Hardly ignorant, in relative terms.

Can you clarify what you mean with more specificity?  Are you claiming that Republicans specifically have a poor understanding of science and politics?  Or is your real claim that Democrats are less ignorant, in general, than Republicans?  Who do you mean when you say "Americans" and to whom are you comparing them?

[1]https://rankingamerica.wordpress.com/category/education/

Is this a cultural problem?  I can point to rapid changes in viewpoints (gay marriage comes to mind) in the last 10-15 years that suggests a certain level of open-mindedness at least on certain topics.

"@isomorphic-drawbridge, I appreciate your detailed response, but I must disagree. The US education rankings, while showing relative success, don't negate the fact that many Americans lack basic scientific and political understanding. It's not about being ignorant, but misinformed due to political polarization and lack of critical thinking. As for your questions, I'm not making a blanket statement about Republicans or Democrats, but rather pointing out that political tribalism often leads to echo chambers and a lack of diverse viewpoints. And when I say 'Americans', I'm comparing them to citizens of other developed nations, where critical thinking and evidence-based reasoning seem more prevalent."

column7:
There's one major difference to this case than your other examples. Gender reassignment doesn't endanger the individual. Telling someone with suicidal tendencies to kill themselves obviously does, and giving lyposuction to someone with body dysmorphia encourages them to continue unhealthy eating habits and be malnourished.

Where does gender reassignment endanger the individual? It's no different than other aesthetically based plastic surgery.

column7:
TRP has the right idea but so many of them take the whole "don't be a nice guy" to extremes that go well into the territory of completely illogical, I can absolutely see why people see them as toxic.

I think you'd have an easy time finding posts that are pretty fucked up, but if you'd like I can find some that I think are fucked up and give you my opinion. Try looking yourself first and apply some of the logic to real life and to the women you know and such.

I explicitly said that a lot of the posts are pretty fucked up. Please correct me if I'm wrong, but I get the impression that you have a response ready that's going to accuse me of misogyny no matter what I say. 

I truly think I have good points, so to you I offer an explanation if you choose to accept it and want to engage in a legit, insult free discussion.
"@joint-light, you're right, I do have a response ready. It's called 'common sense'. 😏"

column7: Oh cool. In which German city are you born?

Actually, Germans don't feel guilt for the actions of the Third Reich anymore - not on a personal level at least, I can't rule it out on a nationalistic level.

Still, we don't forget about them, and we don't *want* to forget about them: While we shouldn't be bound by what has happened in the past, we shouldn't forget it either so that we can avoid to repeat the past faults.

The outrage over the AfD is not that they want to move on from the past, it's that they want to ignore the past (and are seemingly even somewhat open to repeat it).

It's definitely untrue that Germans are taught to not have pride in their country. Maybe you could elaborate? I don't quite get what you want to say with that claim.

For the record, I'm German, so I'm not just commentating on what I've heard from others.

@bland-acre, I get what you're saying about not forgetting the past, but I've seen some German kids in school who were made to feel guilty for something they had nothing to do with. It's like they're being punished for something their grandparents did. That's not fair, is it?

column7:
>Would I be justified in forever being wary around any group that can ever be shown to exhibit such behavior?

YES. 

It is stupid to go against your instincts just to prove that you're "better than that," or something. And don't even try to start with video from the "other side," because I'm sure you know that there are HUNDREDS/THOUSANDS of videos of black-on-white violence readily available on youtube, liveleak, worldstar and whatever other sources.

>Sounds to me like you just want to be racist while pretending that the racism is justified. I've lived in a black neighborhood for 26 years. I've never been assaulted like that. Neither have any of my friends, my parents, or really anyone I know.

Yeah, I don't expect a black guy to be the victim of black-on-white racial violence. If I walked through your neighborhood (assuming it's not some middle-class area), I think it's very likely that I would experience racial violence. 

>You posting a video of a few incidents in a country with 330 million people in it is fairly ridiculous to be honest. I can push just about any narrative if my only standard is that I've caught people acting like idiots on camera.

It's not ridiculous. As I said, there are tons of videos depicting black-on-white racial violence, and crime data too. I live in North Houston, and there's an area nearby which is called Greenspoint, it's a very black area. We call it "Gunspoint" for a reason. Intelligent white people know not to walk around in that area. Poor black neighborhoods are no-go zones for whites. You're the racist if you can't understand that.

First off, I'll say that being called a racist should not more scary to you than getting mugged.  I'm a pretty liberal/progressive guy in reality, but I lived in some bad neighborhoods and there were streets that I would not go down and people I would change my path to avoid.

But I will say that race really doesn't need to factor in to decisions like this.  Race is also an arbitrary and really broad and inaccurate way of grouping people or using as a basis for predicting behavior.  Therefore, trying to judge someone based off of their race when there are other ways of trying to judge them is unreasonable and therefore prejudicial.  I would say income, region (high crime/high poverty), clothing, posture, behavior, etc.  Are far more reasonable ways of pre-judging someone's behavior.

It should be irrelevant to you what race someone is as that's one of the more arbitrary variables here.

You'd maybe be fairly accused of being classist, but at least you'd have a chance to avoid being mugged.

column7: Actually, Bodily Integrity Disorder is not always cured by removing the effective limb. In 30% of patients, the desire transferred to a new limb or part of the body. BIID also responds to therapy that integrates the limb into body image, and psychiatric drugs. Transgender people do not respond to these treatments. While the two disorders look the same, they involve different parts of the brain and different mechanisms.

column7:
Transition regret rates are generally held to fall between 1 and 3 percent. There's higher regret rates for hysterectomies and vasectomies. If I had to guess, the reason for low regret rates for transitioning is that if you've hit a sufficient tipping point for considering such a radical solution then you probably are truly gender dysphoric.

Retention is dysphoria is generally correlated with passability. Transition is never 100 percent effective at removing dysphoria. Rather what youre trying to accomplish is a significant reduction of dysphoric feelings. If hormones take off 20 percent of your dysphoria then FFS surgery knocks out 40 percent then SRS knocks out another 15 percent but you still experience 25 percent dysphoria over broad shoulders and your height, you're still 75 percent more functional and happy than you were.

"@steamed-bulldozer, sounds like you've been reading too many medical journals again 😂. And @active-purse, you're making transitioning sound like a walk in the park, huh? Must be nice to have it all figured out 🙄"

column7: What would you propose as a better alternative to a megathread?

Maybe we need a system where questions are tagged and only unanswered ones show up? That way, we're not flooding the feed but still getting our questions out there.

column7:
You're goal-shifting: the point is that companies can, and have been *known to*, deliberately hide harmful ingredients in the absence of regulation compelling them to do otherwise.  The regulations in question don't necessarily outlaw using those ingredients, but rather using them *without disclosure*.  If this happens, and harms even *one* person, then your claim - "A complete free market and dramatically reducing government spending will improve the lives of ***every*** American" (emphasis mine) - is false.

In formal-logical terms, your claim as stated is equivalent to: `forall(A)[American(a) -> ((freemarket AND reducedgovtspending) -> improvedlife(A))]`, which is equivalent to `notExist(A)[American(A) -> ((freemarket AND reducedgovtspending) -> not(improvedlife(A)))]` (E: In English, "There is no American whose life would not be improved by a complete free market and dramatically reduced government spending"), so demonstrating that an American A, whose life would not be improved by a complete free market and dramatically reduced government spending, exists *is sufficient to disprove your claim*.

It doesn't prove that *most* Americans wouldn't be better off, but it does prove that *not every* American would.

Say an American dies because of the lack of regulations on dangerous substance. Say a preservative made it's way into the mouth of someone who is allergic, because it was not properly tested or disclosed (because the company has no obligation to do so).

Was this American's life improved?

column7:
> My reasoning for being against banning "certain types" of guns is based on the fact that one kind of gun is no more or less lethal than another.

Well, that's just patently false.  Sure, all guns are lethal, but some make the job a hell of a lot easier.  That's really the issue at hand, and I think you do a disservice to the debate by glossing over this.

That said, you're absolutely right about the nonsensical cosmetic bans.  If we're actually interested in reducing guns deaths through firearm restrictions, legislation should focus on functional things like caliber, muzzle velocity, magazine size, etc..

> Even though I had to jump through all those hoops, I never once was required to take a single class in firearm safety, proper maintenance and storage, or even a class on what the laws in my state are regarding the use of a firearm for self defense.

This drives me absolutely fucking nuts.  It varies state-by-state, of course (Arizona doesn't even require a permit for CC), but it's insane to hear people arguing that it's an infringement upon their rights to require safety training (and, God forbid, maybe a bit of marksmanship) before getting a gun, let alone a CC permit.  What I think those who make this argument fail to consider is that they are fighting for the right of every moron with no training to carry and wield a deadly weapon they might accidentally kill a relative or bystander with even if they have no malicious intent.  Prevention is the only effective approach here.  You can't draw down on an accident.
column19: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

column7:
My reasoning for being against banning "certain types" of guns is based on the fact that one kind of gun is no more or less lethal than another.

The assault rifle ban is especially baffling to me, since there is literally no definition to the term. It seems to just mean any firearm in a scary looking stock.

I have a Winchester Varmint Master in .223. Right now it's in a very nice hunting stock, that is perfectly legal. 

Now if I take it out of the hunting stock, and put it in to an armalite frame stock, suddenly it's an illegal weapon.

Mechanically it is the same exact gun, with the same exact ammo capacity, firing the same exact ammunition down the same exact barrel. The only thing that makes it qualify as an "assault rifle" is the scary looking stock.


I can't get behind legislation that bans things for being scary looking. 

Now, and I have said this plenty of times, I have a license to conceal and carry a pistol. to get it I paid $150 for the application, submitted to a federal background check, Provided three character references, interviewed with a sheriff, and then waited a year for my application to be processed.

I think that's a bit much, but I also think there is a VERY important missing step. Even though I had to jump through all those hoops, I never once was required to take a single class in firearm safety, proper maintenance and storage, or even a class on what the laws in my state are regarding the use of a firearm for self defense. 


All those hoops, and they left out the ones that might actually save lives.
column19: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column7: @collinear-pool, I understand your point about certain guns making it easier to cause harm, but I think we're missing the bigger picture here. It's not just about lethality, it's about accessibility and intent. I'm all for better safety training and education, but let's not forget that guns are designed to kill, regardless of their caliber or muzzle velocity. We need to address the root causes of gun violence, not just focus on the tools used.

Feminism isn't "women's advocacy", it's advocacy for people who are oppressed, and in our societies it's women that are oppressed.

Also, a large part of feminist theory is a discussion of what "equality" itself actually entails. Nowadays, to say you're for "equality" means next to nothing.

Also, that which is asserted without evidence can be dismissed without evidence. You provide no evidence to support your claims.

column7:
That's a pretty shallow analysis. If I go to jail for a dime bag of weed (which, by the way, a white person wouldn't), should my girlfriend just disavow me? What if I don't do anything that would qualify me as a "gangster"? What if I accumulate a series of tickets for minor things (which, by the way, a white person wouldn't) and can't take the time off of work to fight the cases and can't afford to pay them and end up getting taken to jail for that? Should people just not procreate with me?

Not buying your interpretation of this that doesn't account for the fact that the system is biased and we all know it is. I'm not going to disown someone I know and love because a biased police officer wanted to make their quota. 

You can break the cycle right now by not blaming people for their own oppression. You're choosing not to.

Generational effects have actually made it so those three things (money, family status, and connections) are all quite concentrated in the white demographic. What you're talking about mostly affects competition *among* white people, but the average white person is better off in all three areas than other groups, this fact is itself *part of* white privilege. This is not changed by the fact that white people don't all have equal amounts of privilege in every area, nor that not every single white person has more of them than every single black person. You're correct that income inequality is a big problem, and the source of many other problems, but are missing the fact that income inequality is itself one of the vectors along which racial inequality makes itself apparent as well.
